elements.sent-2411-001: Structure-based molecule optimization (SBMO) aims to optimize molecules with both continuous coordinates and discrete types against protein targets.
elements.sent-2411-002: A promising direction is to exert gradient guidance on generative models given its remarkable success in images, but it is challenging to guide discrete data and risks inconsistencies between modalities.
elements.sent-2411-003: To this end, we leverage a continuous and differentiable space derived through Bayesian inference, presenting Molecule Joint Optimization (\ours), the first gradient-based SBMO framework that facilitates joint guidance signals across different modalities while preserving SE(3)-equivariance.
elements.sent-2411-004: We introduce a novel backward correction strategy that optimizes within a sliding window of the past histories, allowing for a seamless trade-off between explore-and-exploit during optimization.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our proposed \ours~achieves state-of-the-art performance on CrossDocked2020 benchmark (Success Rate 51.3\%, Vina Dock -9.05 and SA 0.78), more than $4\times$ improvement in Success Rate compared to the gradient-based counterpart, and $2\times$ ``Me-Better'' Ratio as much as 3D baselines.
elements.sent-2411-006: Furthermore, we extend \ours~to a wide range of optimization settings, including multi-objective optimization and challenging tasks in drug design such as R-group optimization and scaffold hopping, further underscoring its versatility and potential.
elements.sent-2411-007: Structure-based drug design (SBDD) plays a critical role in drug discovery by identifying three-dimensional (3D) molecules that are favorable against protein targets \citep{isert_structure-based_2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-008: While recent SBDD focuses on the initial identification of potential drug candidates, these compounds must undergo a series of further modifications for optimized properties, a process that is both complex and time-consuming \citep{hughes2011principles .}
elements.sent-2411-009: Therefore, structure-based molecule optimization (SBMO) has garnered increasing interest in real-world drug design \citep{zhou2024decompopt , emphasizing the practical need of optimizing 3D molecules to meet specific therapeutic criteria.}
elements.sent-2411-010: Concretely, SBMO can be viewed as a more advanced task within the broader scope of general SBDD, requiring precise control over molecular properties while navigating the chemical space.
elements.sent-2411-011: Specifically, SBMO addresses two key aspects: (1) SBMO prioritizes targeted molecular property enhancement according to expert-specified objectives, whereas generative models for SBDD primarily focus on maximizing data likelihood without special emphasis on property improvement \citep{luo_3d_2022, peng_pocket2mol_2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-012: Therefore these models can only produce outputs similar to their training data, limiting the ability to improve molecular properties.
elements.sent-2411-013: The focus on structure makes SBMO particularly suited for key design tasks, such as R-group optimization and scaffold hopping.
elements.sent-2411-014: A pioneering work for SBMO is DecompOpt \citep{zhou2024decompopt , which designs a special 3D generative model conditioned on fragments, and employs a gradient-free sampling method within such decomposed fragment space through iterative oracle calls.}
elements.sent-2411-015: One of the drawbacks is that it relies on oracle functions that can be computationally expensive especially in large-scale SBMO tasks, where running multiple rounds of costly simulations is impractical or even infeasible.
elements.sent-2411-016: Moreover, DecompOpt's contribution lies in its novel generative paradigm based on fragment conditions, rather than exploring optimization within existing models, making it less adaptable to other frameworks.
elements.sent-2411-017: One generalized solution is gradient guidance, addressing these issues by eliminating the need for expensive oracle simulations while being flexible enough to be incorporated into strong-performing generative models in a plug-and-play fashion, as demonstrated in a wide range of challenging real-world applications including image synthesis \citep{dhariwal2021diffusion, epstein2023diffusion .}
elements.sent-2411-018: However, current gradient-based methods have not fully realized their potential in SBMO, for they have historically suffered from the continuous-discrete challenges: (1) it is non-trivial to guide discrete variable within probabilistic generative process.
elements.sent-2411-019: More specifically, standard gradient guidance is designed for continuous variables that follow Gaussian distributions, making them not directly applicable to molecular data that involve discrete atom types.
elements.sent-2411-020: Methods attempting to adapt gradient guidance to discrete data often resort to approximating these variables as continuous, either by adding Gaussian noise \citep{bao2022equivariant or by assuming that classifiers follow a Gaussian distribution \citep{vignac2023digress .}}
elements.sent-2411-021: Unfortunately, these approximations can lead to suboptimal results, as they do not accurately reflect the discrete nature {\citep{pmlr-v202-kong23b .}}
elements.sent-2411-022: For instance, TAGMol \citep{dorna2024tagmol formalizes guidance exclusively over continuous coordinates, resulting in a disconnect between the discrete and continuous modalities.}
elements.sent-2411-023: This may explain why TAGMol struggles to optimize overall molecular properties as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:framework , despite its improvement in Vina affinities.}
elements.sent-2411-024: By solely guiding the continuous coordinates, TAGMol enhances spatial protein-ligand interactions but fails to optimize \eg~synthesizeability, which depends more on molecular topology, especially discrete atom types.
elements.sent-2411-025: In this paper, we address the multi-modality challenge for gradient guidance by leveraging a continuous and differential space, representing an aggregation of noisy samples from the data space derived through Bayesian inference \citep{graves2023bayesian .}
elements.sent-2411-026: We design \ours, a general framework for {\bf{J oint {\bf{O ptimization of 3D molecules and the first proof-of-concept for gradient-based optimization of continuous-discrete variables.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-027: We introduce a novel sampling strategy called backward correction, enhancing the alignment of gradients over different steps.
elements.sent-2411-028: By maintaining a sliding window of past history for optimization, the backward correction strategy enforces explicit dependency on the past, effectively alleviating the issue of inconsistencies.
elements.sent-2411-029: Moreover, it balances the exploration of molecular space with the exploitation of better-aligned guidance signals, offering a flexible trade-off.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{img/framework-all.pdf} \caption{Overview. A. Structure-based molecule optimization, including (1) guiding molecule design by expert-specified objectives, (2) optimizing existing compounds in the structure space. B. Study on the ratio of ``me-better'' molecules, where all other baselines fall short in the overall improvement. C. Overall illustration of \ours, utilizing joint gradient signals over continuous-discrete data, where the distributions of $\btheta$ (continuous $\bmu$ and discrete $\bz$) are taken from true guided trajectories. D. Graphical model of our proposed backward correction strategy, keeping a sliding window of size $k$.} \label{fig:framework}
elements.sent-2411-030: Our main contributions are summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-031: We propose \ours, the first joint gradient-based method for SBMO that establishes the guidance over multi-modality molecular data, offering better controllability and paving the way for gradient guidance over continuous-discrete variables.
elements.sent-2411-032: We design a novel backward correction strategy for effective optimization.
elements.sent-2411-033: By keeping a sliding window and correcting the past given the current optimized version, we achieve better-aligned gradients and facilitate a flexible trade-off between exploration and exploitation.
elements.sent-2411-034: We generalize \ours~to various needs including R-group optimization and scaffold hopping, highlighting its versatility and potential in practical drug design.
elements.sent-2411-035: Pocket-aware generative models aim to learn a conditional distribution over the protein-ligand complex data.
elements.sent-2411-036: Initial approaches adopt 1D SMILES or 2D graph representation \citep{bjerrum2017molecular, gomez2018automatic , and recent research has shifted its focus towards 3D molecule generation in order to better capture the interatomic interactions.}
elements.sent-2411-037: Early atom-based autoregressive models \citep{luo_3d_2022, peng_pocket2mol_2022, liu2022graphbp enforce an atom ordering to generate molecules atom-by-atom.}
elements.sent-2411-038: Fragment-based methods \citep{powers2022fragmentbased, zhang_molecule_2023, lin2023d3fg alleviate the issue of ordering by decomposing molecules into motifs instead of atom-level generation, but they risk more severe error accumulation and thus generally require post-processing or multi-stage treatment.}
elements.sent-2411-039: Non-autoregressive methods based on diffusions \citep{schneuing_structure-based_2022, guan_3d_2023, guan_decompdiff_2023 and BFNs \citep{qu2024molcraft target full-atom generation for enhanced performance and efficiency.}}
elements.sent-2411-040: However, the needs of optimizing certain properties and modifying existing compounds are not adequately addressed in the scope of previous methods, limiting their usefulness in drug design.
elements.sent-2411-041: Inspired by classifier guidance for diffusions \citep{dhariwal2021diffusion , pioneering approaches are committed to adapting the guidance method to handle the complicated molecular geometries in the setting of pocket-unaware generation.}
elements.sent-2411-042: EEGSDE \citep{bao2022equivariant derives an equivariant framework for continuous diffusion, and MUDM \citep{han2023training further explores time-independent property functions for guidance.}}
elements.sent-2411-043: As they enforce a continuous diffusion process for discrete variables, these methods are not applicable in advanced molecular modeling \citep{guan_3d_2023, guan_decompdiff_2023 where discrete data are processed by a discrete diffusion, for it is unnatural to apply progressive Gaussian noise that drives Categorical data away from the simplex.}
elements.sent-2411-044: DiGress \citep{vignac2023digress proposes classifier guidance for discrete diffusion of molecular graphs, yet it additionally assumes that the probability of classifier follows a Gaussian, which is ungrounded and often a problematic approximation.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Based on the continuous-discrete diffusion for SBDD, TAGMol \citep{dorna2024tagmol retains the guidance only for continuous coordinates, because there lacks a proper way to propagate the gradient over discrete types.}
elements.sent-2411-046: The discrete part is affected only implicitly and belatedly in the generative process, and such imbalanced guidance would probably result in suboptimal performance for lack of joint optimization.
elements.sent-2411-047: A protein binding site $\bp = (\bx_P, \bv_P)$ is represented as a point cloud of $N_P$ atoms where atom coordinates $\bx_P=\{\bx_P^{1 , \dots, \bx_P^{N_P}\} \in \bbR^{N_P\times3}$ and $K_P$-dimensional atom features $\bv_P=\{\bv_P^{1 , \dots, \bv_P^{N_P}\} \in \bbR^{N_PK_P}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-048: Similarly, a ligand molecule $\mybm = (\bx_M, \bv_M)$ contains $N_M$ atoms, where $\bx_M^{(i) \in \bbR^3$ is the atomic coordinate and $\bv_M^{(i) \in \bbR^{K_M}$ the atom type.}}
elements.sent-2411-049: For brevity, the subscript for molecules $\cdot_M$ and the pocket condition $\bp$ are omitted unless necessary.
elements.sent-2411-050: Operating in a non-autoregressive fashion, BFN views the generative modeling as message exchange between a sender and a receiver.
elements.sent-2411-051: The sender distribution $p_S(\by~|~\bx; \alpha)$ builds upon the accuracy level $\alpha$ applied to data $\bx$ and defines the noised $\by$.
elements.sent-2411-052: The varying noise levels constitute the schedule $\beta(t) = \int_{t'=0 ^t \alpha(t')dt'$, similar to that in diffusion models.}
elements.sent-2411-053: A key motivation for BFN is that the transmission ought to be continuous and smooth, therefore it does not directly operate on the noisy latent $\by$ as diffusions, but on the structured Bayesian posterior $\btheta$ given noisy latents instead.
elements.sent-2411-054: The receiver holds a prior belief $\btheta_0$, and updates the belief by aggregating noisy observations $\by$, yielding the Bayesian update distribution: \begin{equation}\label{eq:p_U} p_U(\btheta_{i}~|~\btheta_{i-1}, \bx; \alpha_i) = \underset{p_S(\by_i|\bx; \alpha_i)}{\bbE} \delta\Big(\btheta_i - h(\btheta_{i-1}, \by_{i}, \alpha_i)\Big)\end{equation} where $\delta(\cdot)$ is Dirac distribution, and Bayesian update function $h$ is derived through Bayesian inference.
elements.sent-2411-055: Note that $\btheta_i$ can be viewed as the result of mapping given the sender distributions of $\by_0$ to $\by_i$: \begin{equation}\label{eq:mapping} \btheta_i = f(\by_0, \by_1, \dots, \by_i)\end{equation} Intuitively, BFN aims to predict the clean sample given aggregated $\btheta$, \ie~conditioning on all previous latents.
elements.sent-2411-056: The receiver distribution is obtained by marginalizing out $\hat{\bx}$: \begin{equation}\label{eq:receiver} p_R(\by_i~|~\btheta_{i-1}; t_i, \alpha_i) = \underset{p_O(\hat\bx|\bPhi(\btheta_{i-1}, t_i))}{\bbE} p_S(\by_i~|~\hat\bx; \alpha_i)\end{equation} The training objective is to minimize the KL-divergence between sender and receiver distributions: \begin{equation}\label{eq:kl} L^n(\bx) = \underset{\prod_{i=1}^n p_U(\btheta_i | \btheta_{i-1}, \bx; \alpha_i)}{\bbE} \sum_{i=1}^n D_{KL}(p_S(\by_i~|~\bx; \alpha_i)~\Vert~p_R(\by_i~|~\btheta_{i-1}, t_i, \alpha_i)).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-057: In this section, we introduce \ours~that guides the distribution over $\btheta$, utilizing aggregated information from previous latents.
elements.sent-2411-058: Though different from guided diffusions that operate on noisy latent $\by$, this guidance aligns with our generative process conditioned on $\btheta$.
elements.sent-2411-059: By focusing on $\btheta$, we can effectively steer the clean samples towards desirable direction, ensuring a smooth gradient flow.
elements.sent-2411-060: Following \citet{kong2024diffusion and denoting the guided distribution $\pi$ as product of experts \citep{hinton2002training modulated by energy function $E$ that predicts certain property, we have $\pi(\btheta_i | \btheta_{i-1 ) \propto p_\phi(\btheta_i | \btheta_{i-1 )p_E(\btheta_i)$, where $\bPhi$ is the pretrained network for BFN, $p_E(\btheta_i)= \exp{[-E(\btheta_i,t_i)]}$ is the unnormalized Boltzmann distribution corresponding to the time-dependent energy function.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-061: As illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig:framework , we introduce \ours~as follows: in Sec.~\ref{subsec:molbfn , we propose the concept of gradient guidance over the multi-modality molecule space, derive the form of guided transition kernel $\pi(\btheta_i | \btheta_{i-1 )$ via first-order Taylor expansion, and explain the underlying manipulations of distributions the guidance corresponds to.}}}
elements.sent-2411-062: In Sec.~\ref{subsec:guided-flow , we present a generalized advanced sampling strategy termed backward correction for $p_\phi$, which allows for a flexible trade-off between explore-and-exploit by maintaining a sliding window of past histories.}
elements.sent-2411-063: We empirically demonstrate our strategy helps optimize consistency across steps, ultimately improving the overall performance.
elements.subsection-2411-001: Equivariant Guidance for multi-modality Molecular Data
elements.sent-2411-064: In this section, we derive the detailed guidance over $\btheta$ for molecule $\mybm = (\bx, \bv)$ with $N$ atoms, where $\bx \in \bbR^{N\times3}$ represent continuous atom coordinates and $\bv \in \{1, \dots, K \}^N$ for $K$ discrete atom types, and thus $\btheta := [\bmu, \bz]$ for the continuous and discrete modality, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-065: To steer the sampling process towards near-optimal samples, we utilize the score $\nabla_{\btheta}\log p_E(\btheta)$ as a gradient-based property guidance, for which we have the following proposition (proof in Appendix~\ref{proof:core ), followed by details for each modality.}
elements.sent-2411-066: Assuming $\bmu_i\sim \mathcal{N (\bmu_\phi, \sigma)$ and $\by_i\sim \mathcal{N (\by_\phi, \sigma')$ in the original generative process of BFN, we can approximate the guided transition kernel $\pi(\btheta_i|\btheta_{i-1 )$ by sampling \begin{equation}\bmu_i^* \sim\mathcal N(\bmu_\phi+\sigma\bg_{\bmu}, \sigma)\label{eq:samp_first} \by_i^* \sim\mathcal N(\by_\phi+\sigma'\bg_{\by},\sigma')\label{eq:samp_second}\end{equation} where gradient $\bg_{\bmu =-\nabla_{\bmu E(\btheta,t_i)|_{\btheta=\btheta_{i-1 }$, $\bg_{\by =-\nabla_{\by E(\btheta,t_i)|_{\btheta=\btheta_{i-1 }$, recall $\btheta:=[\bmu, \bz=f(\by)]$.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-067: The guidance is formalized over both continuous coordinates and discrete types, and differs from previous guided diffusion for molecules in that (1) it guides the discrete data through Gaussian-distributed latent $\by$ and ensures that the discrete variables are still on the probability simplex without relying on assumptions \citep{vignac2023digress or relaxations \citep{bao2022equivariant, han2023training , and (2) alleviates the inconsistencies between modalities \citep{dorna2024tagmol by joint gradient signals.}}}
elements.sent-2411-068: For continuous coordinates $\bx\in\mathbb{R ^{N\times 3}$, it is natural to adopt a Gaussian sender distribution $\by^x \sim \mathcal{N (\bx, \alpha^{-1}\bold{I )$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-069: With a prior belief $\bmu_0 = \boldsymbol{0}$, we have the Bayesian update function for posterior $\bmu_i$ given noisy $\by^x$ as in \citet{graves2023bayesian : \begin{equation}\label{eq:h_mu} h(\bmu_{i-1}, \by^x, \alpha_i) = \frac{\bmu_{i-1}\rho_{i-1} + \by^x\alpha_i}{\rho_i}\end{equation} with $\alpha_i=\beta_i-\beta_{i-1 , \rho_i=1+\beta_i$ given the schedule $\beta_i=\sigma_1^{-2i/n -1$ for a positive $\sigma_1$ and $n$ steps.}}}
elements.sent-2411-070: In the continuous domain, guidance over $\btheta$ (\ie~$\bmu$) is analogous to guided diffusions, since guiding $\bmu$ corresponds to guiding noisy latent $\by$ using the gradient over it: \begin{equation}\bmu_i^* = \bmu_i + \sigma\bg_{\bmu} = \frac{\bmu_{i-1}\rho_{i-1} + (\by^x+\sigma\frac{\rho_i}{\alpha_i}\bg_{\bmu})\alpha_i}{\rho_i} = \frac{\bmu_{i-1}\rho_{i-1} + (\by^x+\sigma\bg_{\by^x})\alpha_i}{\rho_i}\end{equation} where by chain rule $\bg_{\by^x = -\nabla_{\by^x E(\btheta, t_i)|_{\btheta=\btheta_{i-1 = \bg_{\bmu}\frac{\partial h {\partial \by^x = \bg_{\bmu}\frac{\alpha_i {\rho_i}$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-071: This builds a connection between the guidance over $\btheta$ and over latent $\by$ for continuous data.
elements.sent-2411-072: For $N$-dimensional discrete types $\bv \in \{1, \dots, K \}^N$, the noisy latent represents the counts of each type among $K$ types, where we have $\by^v \sim \mathcal{N (\by^v | \alpha'(K\be_{\bv -\bold{1 ), \alpha' K\bold{I )$, $\be_{\bv = [\be_{\bv^{(1) , \dots, \be_{\bv^{(N) ] \in \bbR^{KN}$, $\be_{\bv^{(j) =\delta_{\bv^{(j) \in \bbR^{K}$ with Kronecker delta function $\delta$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-073: This is mathematically grounded with further explanation in Appendix~\ref{sec:app-sender-discrete .}
elements.sent-2411-074: The aggregated $\bz_i$ as a posterior belief is updated given the prior $\bz_0=\frac{\bold{1 {\bold{K }$ as: \begin{equation}\label{eq:h_z} h(\bz_{i-1}, \by^v, \alpha'_i) = \frac{\exp(\by^v)\bz_{i-1}}{\sum_{k=1}^{K}\exp(\by^v_k)(\bz_{i-1})_k}\end{equation} where the redundant $\alpha'_i=\beta'_i-\beta'_{i-1}$ with $\beta'_i=\beta'_1(\frac{i {n )^2$, given a positive hyperparameter $\beta'_1$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-075: In the discrete domain, guiding all latents $\by$ amounts to a reweight of the Categorical distribution for $\btheta$ (\ie~$\bz$), changing the probability of each class in accordance with the gradient.
elements.sent-2411-076: Take an extreme case to illustrate, where $\bg_{\by^v}$ is filled with one-hot vectors $\delta_d$: \begin{equation}(\bz^*_i)_{k} = \frac{\exp(\by^v_k)(\bz_{i-1})_k}{\sum_{k'=1}^{K}\exp(\by^v_{k'})(\bz_{i-1})_{k'} + [\exp(\sigma')-1]\exp(\by^v_d)(\bz_{i-1})_d} = (\bz_i)_{k}\frac{1}{1+C} < (\bz_i)_k\end{equation} for all $k \ne d$, where $C=[\exp(\sigma')-1]\exp(\by^v_d)(\bz_{i-1 )_d/[{\sum_{k'=1 ^{K}\exp(\by^v_{k' ) ] > 0$ as the variance $\sigma'>0$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-077: It is obvious that the guidance lowers the probability for all classes but the favored $d$, redistributing the mass for discrete data in a more structured way than diffusion counterparts.
elements.sent-2411-078: Our proposed guided sampling that utilizes joint gradient signals is still equivariant as shown in the proposition below, with proof in Appendix~\ref{proof:equi .}
elements.sent-2411-079: The guided sampling process preserves SE(3)-equivariance when $\bPhi$ is SE(3)-equivariant, if the energy function $E(\btheta, \bp, t)$ is also parameterized with an SE(3)-equivariant neural network, and the complex is shifted to the space where the protein's Center of Mass (CoM) is zero.
elements.subsection-2411-002: Guided Bayesian Update With Backward Correction
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Gradient Guided Sampling of \ours~with Backward Correction}\label{algo:sample} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \REQUIRE network $\bPhi(\btheta, t, \bp)$, noise schedules $[\beta(t), \beta'(t)]$, number of sample steps $n$, back correction steps $k$, number of atom types $K$, energy function $E(\btheta,\bp, t)$, guidance scale $s$ \STATE Initialize belief $\btheta :=[\bmu, \bz] \gets [\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{\frac{1}{K}}]$ \COMMENT{~Continuous aggregations for different modalities} \FOR{$i=1$ to $n$} \STATE $[t, t_{-k}] \gets [\frac{i-1}{n}, \max(0, \frac{i-k-1}{n})]$ \COMMENT{~Discrete timesteps in $[0,1)$} \STATE $[\hat{\bx}, \hat{\be}_{\bv}] \gets \bPhi(\btheta, t, \bp)$ \COMMENT{~Network predicts clean data} \STATE $[\bg_{\bmu},\bg_{\by}]\gets [-\nabla_{\bmu}E( \btheta,\bp,t), -\nabla_{\by}E( \btheta,\bp,t)]$ \COMMENT{~Gradient guidance} \STATE $[\rho, \rho_{-k}, \Delta\beta, \Delta\beta'] \gets [1+\beta(t), 1+\beta(t_{-k}), \beta(t)-\beta(t_{-k}), \beta'(t) - \beta'(t_{-k})]$ \STATE {Retrieve $\bmu_{-k}, \bz_{-k}$ from the past} \COMMENT{~Backward correction starting point} \STATE {Sample $\bmu \sim \mathcal N([\frac{\rho(\Delta\beta\hat\bx+\bmu_{-k}\rho_{-k}) + s\Delta\beta\bg_{\bmu}}{\rho^2}, \frac{\Delta\beta}{\rho^2}\mathbf{I})$} \COMMENT{~Guidance for continuous $\bx$} \STATE {Sample $\by \sim \mathcal N(\Delta\beta'(t)(K\hat{\be}_{\bv} - \mathbf{1} + sK\bg_{\by}), \Delta\beta'(t)K\mathbf{I})$} \COMMENT{~Guidance for discrete $\bv$} \STATE {Update $\bz \gets \frac{\exp(\by)\bz_{-k}}{\sum_{i=1}^{K}\exp(\by_i)(\bz_{-k})_i}$ } \COMMENT{~Aggregate latents for discrete data} \ENDFOR \STATE $[\hat{\bx}, \hat{\be}_{\bv}] \gets \bPhi(\btheta,1,\bp)$ \COMMENT{~Network predicts final output} \STATE $\hat{\bv} \gets \arg\max(\hat{\be}_{\bv})$ \COMMENT{~Final discrete output from p.m.f.} \RETURN $[\hat{\bx}, \hat{\bv}]$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-080: Here we propose a general backward correction sampling strategy inspired from the optimization perspective, and analyze its effect on aligning the gradients.
elements.sent-2411-081: Recall that from Eq.~\ref{eq:p_U we can aggregate $\btheta_i$ based on latents from the previous step: \begin{equation}\label{eq:no_correction} p_\phi(\btheta_i~|~\btheta_{i-1}) = \underset{p_O(\hat\bx_{i}|\bPhi(\btheta_{i-1}, t_i))}{\bbE}p_U(\btheta_{i}~|~\btheta_{i-1}, \hat\bx_i; \alpha_i)\end{equation} Backward correction aims at ``correcting the past to further optimize''.}
elements.sent-2411-082: Since we obtain an optimized $\btheta_i^*$ from the guided kernel $\pi(\btheta_i|\btheta_{i-1 )$, there will be an optimized version of $\hat\bx_i^*=\hat\bx_{i+1}$ for the next step.}
elements.sent-2411-083: By backward correcting the Bayesian update distribution $p_U$ given the optimized $\hat\bx^*$, we are able to reinforce the current best possible parameter $\btheta$, instead of building on the suboptimal history.
elements.sent-2411-084: By utilizing the property of additive accuracy once $p_U$ follows certain form as described by \citet{graves2023bayesian , the one-step backward correction can be derived as follows: \begin{equation}p_\phi(\btheta_i~|~\btheta_{i-1},\btheta_{i-2}) = \underset{p_O(\hat\bx_{i}|\bPhi(\btheta_{i-1}, t_i))}{\bbE} {\underset{p_U(\btheta_{i-1}|\btheta_{i-2}, \callout{\color{red}{\hat\bx_i}} {originally ${\color{red}\hat\bx_{i-1}}\sim p_O(\hat\bx_{i-1}|\bPhi(\btheta_{i-2}, t_{i-1}))$}; \alpha_{i-1})}{\bbE}} p_U(\btheta_{i}~|~\btheta_{i-1}, \hat\bx_i; \alpha_{i}) \nonumber = \underset{p_O(\hat\bx_{i}|\bPhi(\btheta_{i-1}, t_i))}{\bbE} p_U(\btheta_{i}~|~\btheta_{i-2}, \hat\bx_i; \alpha_{i-1}+\alpha_{i})\end{equation} and we arrive at the $k-1$ step corrected estimation of $p_\phi$: \begin{equation}p_\phi(\btheta_n|\btheta_{n-1}, \btheta_{n-k}) = \underset{p_O(\hat\bx_{n}|\bPhi(\btheta_{n-1}, t_n))}{\bbE}p_U(\btheta_n | \btheta_{n-k}, \hat\bx_n; \sum_{i=n-k+1}^n\alpha_i)\label{eq:correction_k}\end{equation} Plugging Eq.~\ref{eq:h_mu and \ref{eq:h_z together with the sender distributions defined above into the right hand side according to Eq.~\ref{eq:p_U , yields the analytic form of the backward corrected Bayesian update \begin{equation}\label{eq:p_U_mu} p_U(\bmu_n | \bmu_{n-k}, \hat\bx_n) = N\Big( \frac{\Delta\beta \hat\bx_n + \bmu_{n-k}\rho_{n-k}}{\rho_n}, \frac{\Delta\beta}{\rho_n^2}\bold{I} \Big) \label{eq:p_U_z} p_U(\bz_n | \bz_{n-k}, \hat\bv_n) = \underset{\by \sim N(\by | \Delta\beta'(K\be_{\hat\bv_n}-\bold{1}), \Delta\beta' K\bold{I})}{\bbE} \delta\Big( \bz_n - \frac{\exp(\by)\bz_{n-k}}{\sum_{i=1}^{K}\exp(\by_i)(\bz_{n-k})_i} \Big)\end{equation} where $\hat\mybm=[\hat{\bx , \hat{\bv ]$ is drawn from the output distribution $p_O({\hat\mybm ~|~\bPhi(\btheta_{i-1 , t_{i-1 , \bp))$ given pocket $\bp$, $\Delta\beta=\beta_n-\beta_{n-k}$ and $\Delta\beta'=\beta'_n-\beta'_{n-k}$ are obtained from corresponding schedules.}}}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-011: \begin{equation}p_\phi(\btheta_i~|~\btheta_{i-1},\btheta_{i-2}) = \underset{p_O(\hat\bx_{i}|\bPhi(\btheta_{i-1}, t_i))}{\bbE} {\underset{p_U(\btheta_{i-1}|\btheta_{i-2}, \callout{\color{red}{\hat\bx_i}} {originally ${\color{red}\hat\bx_{i-1}}\sim p_O(\hat\bx_{i-1}|\bPhi(\btheta_{i-2}, t_{i-1}))$}; \alpha_{i-1})}{\bbE}} p_U(\btheta_{i}~|~\btheta_{i-1}, \hat\bx_i; \alpha_{i}) \nonumber = \underset{p_O(\hat\bx_{i}|\bPhi(\btheta_{i-1}, t_i))}{\bbE} p_U(\btheta_{i}~|~\btheta_{i-2}, \hat\bx_i; \alpha_{i-1}+\alpha_{i})\end{equation}

elements.sent-2411-136: In this section, we provide a further explanation of Bayesian Flow Networks (BFNs) that are designed to model the generation of data through a process of message exchange between a ``sender'' and a ``receiver'' \citep{graves2023bayesian .}
elements.sent-2411-137: The fundamental elements include the sender distribution for the sender, and input distribution, output distribution, receiver distribution for the receiver.
elements.sent-2411-138: This process is framed within the context of Bayesian inference, where the sender distribution is a factorized distribution $p_S(\by|\mybm,\alpha_t)$ that introduces noise to each dimension of the data $\mybm$ and sends it to the receiver, which observes the $\by$ and has access to the noisy channel with accuracy $\alpha$ at timestep $t$, and compares it with its own receiver distribution $p_R(\by|\btheta, \mathbf{p ; t)$ based on its current belief of the parameters $\btheta$, the timestep and any conditional input such as protein pocket $\bp$.}
elements.sent-2411-139: The generative process for the receiver begins with a prior distribution (referred to as input distribution $p_I(\mybm | \btheta)= \prod_{d=1 ^N p_I(m^{(d) | \theta^{(d) )$ that is also factorized for $N$-dimensional data) that defines its initial belief about the data.}}}
elements.sent-2411-140: For continuous data, the prior can be chosen as a Gaussian \citep{song2024unified, qu2024molcraft , or other distribution such as von Mises distribution \citep{anonymous2024a , while for discrete data, the prior is modeled as a uniform categorical distribution.}}
elements.sent-2411-141: Then, the receiver uses its belief $\btheta$ with the help of neural network to model the inter-dependency among dimensions and compute the output distribution $p_O(\hat{\bold{m |\btheta, \mathbf{p ; t)$, which represents its estimate of the possible reconstructions of original data $\mybm$, and is used to construct the receiver distribution (Eq.~\ref{eq:receiver ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-142: The Bayesian update function in Eq.~\ref{eq:p_U defines how the prior belief $\btheta_0$ is updated to the conjugate posterior $\btheta_t$.}
elements.sent-2411-143: Ideally, the update requires aggregating all possible noisy latents $\by$ from the sender distribution $p_S$, while during the actual generative process, there is only receiver distribution $p_R$, leaving an exposure bias, and different approximations determine different forms of mapping $f$ to the posterior (Eq.~\ref{eq:mapping ), showcasing the flexibility in the design space of BFN.}
elements.sent-2411-144: Through the iterative communication between sender and receiver, the receiver progressively updates its belief of the underlying parameters, and training is achieved by minimizing the divergence between the sender and receiver distributions (Eq.~\ref{eq:kl ).}
elements.sent-2411-145: This is analogous to the way Bayesian inference works in parameter estimation: as more noisy data $\by$ is observed, the receiver's posterior belief about the data $\mybm$ becomes increasingly accurate, which implies the reconstruction would be made easier.

elements.sent-2411-186: We provide a detailed description of all baselines here:.
elements.sent-2411-187: AR \citep{luo_3d_2022 uses MCMC sampling to reconstruct a molecule atom-by-atom given voxel-wise densities.}
elements.sent-2411-188: GraphBP \citep{liu2022graphbp is an autoregressive atom-based model that uses normalizing flow and encodes the context to preserve 3D geometric equivariance.}
elements.sent-2411-189: Pocket2Mol \citep{peng_pocket2mol_2022 generates one atom with bond at a time via an E(3)-equivariant network.}
elements.sent-2411-190: It predicts frontier atoms to expand, alleviating the efficiency problem in sampling.
elements.sent-2411-191: FLAG \citep{zhang_molecule_2023 is a fragment-based model that assembles the generated fragments via predicted coordinates and torsion angles.}
elements.sent-2411-192: DiffSBDD \citep{schneuing_structure-based_2022 constructs an equivariant continuous diffusion for full-atom generation given pocket information, and applies Gaussian noise to both continuous atom coordinates and discrete atom types.}
elements.sent-2411-193: TargetDiff \citep{guan_3d_2023 adopts a continuous-discrete diffusion approach that treats each modality via corresponding diffusion process, achieving better performance than continuous diffusion such as DiffSBDD.}
elements.sent-2411-194: DecompDiff \citep{guan_decompdiff_2023 decomposes the molecules into contact arms and linking scaffolds, and utilizes such chemical priors in the diffusion process.}
elements.sent-2411-195: IPDiff \citep{huang2024protein pretrains an affinity predictor, and utilizes this predictor to extract features that augments the conditioning of diffusion generative process.}
elements.sent-2411-196: MolCRAFT \citep{qu2024molcraft employs Bayesian Flow Network for molecular design with an advanced sampling strategy, showing notable improvement upon diffusion counterparts.}
elements.sent-2411-197: RGA \citep{fu2022reinforced is an optimization method that utilizes 1D SMILES representation and traverses the molecular space via mutation-based genetic algorithm.}
elements.sent-2411-198: Starting from a number of seed molecules as initialization, RGA iteratively makes oracle calls for docking simulation feedback, and keeps the top-scoring molecules in the end.
elements.sent-2411-199: DecompOpt \citep{zhou2024decompopt trains a conditional generative model on decomposed fragments besides binding pocket, following the style of DecompDiff.}
elements.sent-2411-200: The optimization is done by iteratively re-sampling in the 3D diffusion latent space given the top $K$ arms ranked by oracle functions as updated fragment condition input.
elements.sent-2411-201: TAGMol \citep{dorna2024tagmol exerts gradient-based property guidance over the pretrained TargetDiff backbone, and the gradient is enabled only in the continuous diffusion process for coordinates.}
elements.sent-2411-202: Besides the common evaluation metrics such as binding affinities calculated by Autodock Vina \citep{eberhardt2021autodock and QED, SA by RDKit, we elaborate other metrics as follows:.}
elements.sent-2411-203: Diversity measures the diversity of generated molecules for each binding site.
elements.sent-2411-204: Following SBDD convention \citep{luo_3d_2022 , it is based on Tanimoto similarity over Morgan fingerprints, and averaged on 100 test proteins.}
elements.sent-2411-205: Connected Ratio is the ratio of complete molecules among all generated molecules, \ie~with only one connected component.
elements.sent-2411-206: Lipinski enumerates Lipinski rule of five \citep{lipinski1997experimental and checks how many rules are satisfied.}
elements.sent-2411-207: These rules are typically seen as empirical reference that helps to predict whether the molecule is likely to be orally bioavailable.
elements.sent-2411-208: Key Interaction, \ie~key non-covalent interactions formed between molecules and protein binding sites as an in-depth measure for binding modes, including {$\pi$ interactions , {hydrogen bonds (donor and acceptor), {salt bridges and {hydrophobic interactions calculated by Schr\"odinger Glide \citep{halgren2004glide .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-209: Strain Energy measures the internal energy of generated poses, serving as an indicator of pose quality as proposed by \cite{harris2023benchmarking .}
elements.sent-2411-210: Steric Clash calculates the number of clashes between generated ligand and protein surface, where clashing means the distance of ligand and protein atoms are within a certain threshold.

elements.sent-2411-262: Future directions include extending the general framework to accommodate a wider range of objectives, and even beyond the scope of structure-based molecule optimization (SBMO), for \ours~is a general gradient-based optimation method for continuous and discrete variables, and can be tailored to a great many read-world applications.
elements.sent-2411-263: To this end, we leverage a continuous and differentiable space derived through Bayesian inference, presenting Molecule Joint Optimization (\ours), the gradient-based SBMO framework that facilitates joint guidance signals across different modalities while preserving SE(3)-equivariance.
elements.sent-2411-264: Furthermore, we extend \ours~to a wide range of optimization settings, including multi-objective optimization and challenging tasks in drug design such as R-group optimization and scaffold hopping, further underscoring its versatility.
elements.sent-2411-265: Structure-based drug design (SBDD) plays a critical role in drug discovery by identifying three-dimensional (3D) molecules that are favorable to protein targets \citep{isert_structure-based_2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-266: Therefore, structure-based molecule optimization (SBMO) has garnered increasing interest in real-world drug design \citep{zhou2024decompopt , emphasizing the practical need to optimize 3D molecules to meet specific therapeutic criteria.}
elements.sent-2411-267: Specifically, SBMO addresses two key aspects: (1) SBMO prioritizes targeted molecular property enhancement according to expert-specified objectives, while generative models for SBDD focus primarily on maximizing the likelihood of data without special emphasis on property improvement \citep{luo_3d_2022, peng_pocket2mol_2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-268: Therefore, these models can only produce outputs similar to their training data, limiting the ability to improve molecular properties.
elements.sent-2411-269: Recent works have explored SBMO through evolutionary-based resampling and gradient-based methods.
elements.sent-2411-270: For instance, DiffSBDD \citep{schneuing_structure-based_2022 uses a gradient-free evolutionary sampling method, and DecompOpt \citep{zhou2024decompopt further introduces a fragment-conditioned 3D generative model for resampling.}}

elements.sent-2411-283: The receiver distribution is obtained by marginalizing out $\hat{\bx}$: \begin{equation}\label{eq:receiver} p_R(\by_i~|~\btheta_{i-1}; t_i, \alpha_i) = \underset{p_O(\hat\bx|\bPhi(\btheta_{i-1}, t_i))}{\bbE} p_S(\by_i~|~\hat\bx; \alpha_i)\end{equation} The training objective is to minimize the KL-divergence between sender and receiver distributions: \begin{equation}\label{eq:kl} L^n(\bx) = \underset{\prod_{i=1}^n p_U(\btheta_i | \btheta_{i-1}, \bx; \alpha_i)}{\bbE} \nonumber \sum_{i=1}^nD_{KL} (p_S(\by_i~|~\bx; \alpha_i)~\Vert~p_R(\by_i~|~\btheta_{i-1}, t_i, \alpha_i)).\end{equation}.

elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Gradient Guided Sampling of \ours}\label{algo:sample} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \REQUIRE network $\bPhi(\btheta, t, \bp)$, schedules $[\beta^x(t), \beta^v(t)]$, number of sample steps $n$, back correction steps $k$, number of atom types $K$, energy function $E(\btheta,\bp, t)$, guidance scale $s$ \STATE Initialize belief $\btheta :=[\btheta^x, \btheta^v] \gets [\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{\frac{1}{K}}]$ \FOR{$i=1$ to $n$} \STATE $[t, t_{-k}] \gets [\frac{i-1}{n}, \max(0, \frac{i-k-1}{n})]$ \STATE $[\hat{\bx}, \hat{\be}_{\bv}] \gets \bPhi(\btheta, t, \bp)$ \STATE $[\bg_{\btheta^x},\bg_{\btheta^v}]\gets [-\nabla_{\btheta^x}E( \btheta,\bp,t), -\nabla_{\btheta^v}E( \btheta,\bp,t)]$ \STATE {$[\rho, \rho_{-k}, \Delta\beta^x, \Delta\beta^v] \gets [1+\beta^x(t), 1+\beta^x(t_{-k}), \beta^x(t)-\beta^x(t_{-k}), \beta^v(t) - \beta^v(t_{-k})]$} \STATE {Retrieve $\btheta^x_{-k}, \btheta^v_{-k}$ from the past} \STATE {Sample $\btheta^x$ according to Eq.~\ref{eq:samp_first} and \ref{eq:p_U_mu}} \STATE {Sample $\by^v$ and update $\btheta^v$ according to Eq.~\ref{eq:samp_second} and \ref{eq:p_U_z}} \ENDFOR \STATE $[\hat{\bx}, \hat{\be}_{\bv}] \gets \bPhi(\btheta,1,\bp)$ \STATE $\hat{\bv} \gets \arg\max(\hat{\be}_{\bv})$ \RETURN {$[\hat{\bx}, \hat{\bv}]$} \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-297: Recall that from Eq.~\ref{eq:p_U we can aggregate $\btheta_i$ from previous latents: \begin{equation}\label{eq:no_correction} p_\phi(\btheta_i~|~\btheta_{i-1}) = \underset{p_O(\hat\bx_{i}|\bPhi(\btheta_{i-1}, t_i))}{\bbE}p_U(\btheta_{i}~|~\btheta_{i-1}, \hat\bx_i; \alpha_i)\end{equation} Backward correction aims at ``correcting the past to further optimize''.}
elements.sent-2411-298: By utilizing the property of additive accuracy once $p_U$ follows certain form as described by \citet{graves2023bayesian , the one-step backward correction can be derived as follows: \begin{equation}p_\phi(\btheta_i~|~\btheta_{i-1},\btheta_{i-2}) = \nonumber \underset{p_O(\hat\bx_{i}|\bPhi(\btheta_{i-1}, t_i))}{\bbE} {\underset{p_U(\btheta_{i-1}|\btheta_{i-2}, {\color{red}{\hat\bx_i}} ; \alpha_{i-1})}{\bbE}} p_U(\btheta_{i}~|~\btheta_{i-1}, \hat\bx_i; \alpha_{i}) \nonumber = \underset{p_O(\hat\bx_{i}|\bPhi(\btheta_{i-1}, t_i))}{\bbE} p_U(\btheta_{i}~|~\btheta_{i-2}, \hat\bx_i; \alpha_{i-1}+\alpha_{i})\end{equation} where the original $\hat{\bx _{i-1}\sim p_O(\hat\bx_{i-1 |\bPhi(\btheta_{i-2 , t_{i-1 ))$ that has been used to update $\btheta_{i-1}$ from $\btheta_{i-2}$ at previous step, is now replaced by the optimized $\hat{\bx _i$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-299: By iteratively tracing back, we arrive at the $k-1$ step corrected estimation of $p_\phi$: \begin{equation}p_\phi(\btheta_n|\btheta_{n-1}, \btheta_{n-k}) = \nonumber \underset{p_O(\hat\bx_{n}|\bPhi(\btheta_{n-1}, t_n))}{\bbE} p_U(\btheta_n | \btheta_{n-k}, \hat\bx_n; \sum_{i=n-k+1}^n\alpha_i)\label{eq:correction_k}\end{equation} Plugging Eq.~\ref{eq:h_mu and \ref{eq:h_z together with the sender distributions defined above into the right hand side according to Eq.~\ref{eq:p_U , yields the form of the backward corrected Bayesian update \begin{equation}\label{eq:p_U_mu} p_U(\btheta^x_n | \btheta^x_{n-k}, \hat\bx_n) = N\Big( \frac{\Delta\beta \hat\bx_n + \btheta^x_{n-k}\rho_{n-k}}{\rho_n}, \frac{\Delta\beta}{\rho_n^2}\bold{I} \Big) \label{eq:p_U_z} p_U(\btheta^v_n | \btheta^v_{n-k}, \hat\bv_n) = \underset{\by \sim N(\by | \Delta\beta^v(K\be_{\hat\bv_n}-\bold{1}), \Delta\beta^v K\bold{I})}{\bbE} \nonumber \delta\Big( \btheta^v_n - \frac{\exp(\by)\btheta^v_{n-k}}{\sum_{i=1}^{K}\exp(\by_i)(\btheta^v_{n-k})_i} \Big)\end{equation} where $\hat\mybm=[\hat{\bx , \hat{\bv ]$ is drawn from the output distribution $p_O({\hat\mybm |\bPhi(\btheta_{i-1 , t_{i-1 , \bp))$ given pocket $\bp$, $\Delta\beta^x=\beta^x_n-\beta^x_{n-k}$ and $\Delta\beta^v=\beta^v_n-\beta^v_{n-k}$ are obtained from corresponding accuracy schedules.}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-322: We present \ours, the joint gradient-based SE(3)-equivariant framework to solve the structure-based molecule optimization problems, which only requires differentiable energy functions instead of expensive oracle simulations.

elements.sent-2411-386: In this section, we provide further explanation of Bayesian Flow Networks (BFNs) that are designed to model the generation of data through a process of message exchange between a ``sender'' and a ``receiver'' \citep{graves2023bayesian .}
